1: Chapter 25 provides a programmatic summary of Jeremiah's ministry at a pivotal historical moment: the word that came to Jeremiah concerning all the people of Judah, in the fourth year of Jehoiakim the son of Josiah, king of Judah (that was the first year of Nebuchadnezzar king of Babylon). The convergence of dates is significant: 605 BC is when Nebuchadnezzar defeated Egypt at Carchemish and effectively became the dominant power in the ancient Near East. This is the moment when the abstract north-foe of Jeremiah's earlier oracles becomes a historically identified, present military reality.
2: Jeremiah the prophet spoke to all the people of Judah and all the inhabitants of Jerusalem. The audience is universal within Judah — not just the Temple crowd or the royal court but the entire population of the nation and capital. The scope of the address matches the scope of the indictment: everyone has been involved in the covenant violation, and everyone will be subject to the coming judgment.
3: For twenty-three years, from the thirteenth year of Josiah the son of Amon, king of Judah, to this day, the word of YHWH has come to me, and I have spoken persistently to you, but you have not listened. Jeremiah's ministry began in 627 BC, the thirteenth year of Josiah. Twenty-three years of preaching — through Josiah's reform, through Josiah's death, through Jehoahaz, through Jehoiakim — and the people have not listened. The inventory of faithful prophecy unheeded makes the coming judgment all the more inexorable: YHWH has exhausted the ordinary channels of covenant communication.
4: You have neither listened nor inclined your ears to hear, although YHWH persistently sent to you all his servants the prophets. The full prophetic tradition is invoked: not just Jeremiah but the entire succession of covenant messengers YHWH sent before him. Persistently sent (hashkem veshloach, rising early and sending) — the idiom of diligent, urgent effort. YHWH did not send prophets occasionally; he sent them relentlessly, rising early in his urgency to communicate the covenant message. The people consistently refused to incline their ears.
5: They said, Turn now, every one of you, from his evil way and evil deeds, and dwell upon the land that YHWH has given to you and your fathers from of old and forever. The content of the prophets' message was simple: repent (shuvu, turn) and remain in the covenant land. The land was given from of old (me'olam) and forever (ad-olam) — the unconditional Abrahamic promise of the land. But the conditional Sinaitic covenant determines whether the current generation occupies it.
6: Do not go after other gods to serve and worship them, or provoke me to anger with the work of your hands. Then I will do you no harm. The covenant demand in its simplest form: one God, no idols. Provoke me to anger with the work of your hands (lehach'iseni bema'aseh yedekhem) — idol worship is an act of deliberate provocation, not innocent religious diversity. The promise I will do you no harm frames the entire warning negatively: the prophetic message has been mercy all along, offering escape from the judgment that the people's own choices are drawing down on themselves.
7: Yet you have not listened to me, declares YHWH, that you might provoke me to anger with the work of your hands to your own harm. The irony of self-destructive rebellion: by refusing to listen, they have provoked YHWH to their own harm (lema'an hara' lachem). They thought idol-worship served their interests — security, fertility, political alignment with the dominant cultures. They were wrong: it was harm to themselves, guaranteed.
8: Therefore thus says YHWH of hosts: Because you have not obeyed my words. The therefore (lachen) is the pivot from indictment to judgment — the logical-covenantal connection between the people's disobedience and YHWH's response. The judgment that follows is not arbitrary; it is the consequence directly connected to the cause.
9: Behold, I will send for all the tribes of the north, declares YHWH, and for Nebuchadnezzar the king of Babylon, my servant, and I will bring them against this land and its inhabitants, and against all these surrounding nations. I will devote them to destruction, and make them a horror, a hissing, and an everlasting desolation. Nebuchadnezzar my servant (avdi) — one of the most remarkable designations in the entire book. Eved (servant) is the term for Israel's covenant mediators: Moses was YHWH's servant (Deuteronomy 34:5), David was YHWH's servant (2 Samuel 7:5), the prophets are YHWH's servants (Jeremiah 7:25; Jeremiah 25:4). Now the pagan king of Babylon receives the same title — not as a convert but as an instrument of covenant judgment. YHWH's sovereignty over history extends to using unbelieving empires for covenantal purposes.
10: And I will banish from them the voice of mirth and the voice of gladness, the voice of the bridegroom and the voice of the bride, the grinding of the millstones and the light of the lamp. The silencing of Jerusalem catalogues the sounds and lights of normal daily life: wedding celebration (cf. Jeremiah 7:34; Jeremiah 16:9 — the returning voice of bride and bridegroom marks restoration in Jeremiah 33:11), grain-grinding (the rhythm of domestic food preparation), lamplight (the sign of inhabited homes). All will be extinguished. The city that was full of the sounds of life will become silent as a tomb.
11: This whole land shall become a ruin and a waste, and these nations shall serve the king of Babylon seventy years. The seventy years oracle is one of the most discussed chronological statements in the Hebrew Bible. Seventy was a round number of conventional completeness in the ancient Near East — a full human lifespan (Psalms 90:10), a number associated with divine deliberations (Deuteronomy 32:8 — seventy nations), and a period long enough to encompass multiple generations. An Esarhaddon inscription describes Babylon's desolation by Sennacherib in 689 BC as a seventy-year punishment (later numerically inverted to eleven years). The seventy years functioned as a symbol of thorough, complete judgment that would outlast the generation that caused it.
12: Then after seventy years are completed, I will punish the king of Babylon and that nation, the land of the Chaldeans, for their iniquity, declares YHWH, making the land an everlasting waste. The judgment of Babylon is announced from the outset: YHWH's use of Babylon as his instrument does not exempt Babylon from accountability for its own cruelty and pride. The Babylonian empire that serves YHWH's purposes will itself face YHWH's judgment when its instrument-role is complete. This dual structure — Babylon as servant and Babylon as sinner — runs through chapters 50–51 where the oracles against Babylon are concentrated.
13: I will bring upon that land all the words that I have uttered against it, everything written in this book, which Jeremiah prophesied against all the nations. The reference to this book (hasefer hazeh) suggests that by the time of chapter 25's composition/compilation, a written collection of Jeremiah's oracles already existed. The oracles against the nations (chapters 46–51) are thus anticipated here. The judgment of Babylon brings the entire written prophetic corpus to completion: what was written will be executed.
14: For many nations and great kings shall make slaves even of them, and I will recompense them according to their deeds and the work of their hands. Babylon's fall is described as subjugation: the great empire that made slaves of nations will itself become enslaved. The covenant-recompense formula (according to their deeds and work of their hands) is applied universally — the standard that judged Judah's idolatry will judge Babylon's imperial cruelty.
15: Thus YHWH, the God of Israel, said to me: Take from my hand this cup of the wine of wrath, and make all the nations to whom I send you drink it. The cup of wrath oracle (vv15–29) is one of the most vivid prophetic sign-acts in the book — though whether Jeremiah literally carried a cup to each nation or the action was visionary and symbolic is debated. The cup of YHWH's wrath (kos hachemah) is a judicial image: the defeated king is forced by the victor to drink the cup of defeat and humiliation. YHWH as cosmic king forces the nations to drink his judgment.
16: They shall drink and stagger and be crazed because of the sword that I am sending among them. The wine of wrath produces not intoxicating pleasure but disorienting madness (hitgale'lu vehitholalu, stagger and act like madmen) — a picture of armies and peoples thrown into confusion and panic by YHWH's judgment. The sword sent among them is Babylon, now YHWH's instrument for universal judgment.
17: So I took the cup from YHWH's hand, and made all the nations to whom YHWH sent me drink it. Jeremiah's obedient reception and administration of the cup follows the typical sign-act pattern: YHWH commands, Jeremiah executes. The prophet's role as YHWH's agent in distributing judgment mirrors his role as messenger of the divine word — both activities make the divine will concrete in the world.
18: Jerusalem and the cities of Judah, its kings and officials, to make them a desolation and a waste, a hissing and a curse, as at this day. Jerusalem and Judah are listed first among the cup-recipients — judgment begins with the house of YHWH (cf. 1 Peter 4:17; Ezekiel 9:6). The phrase as at this day suggests a post-587 perspective — the narrator is looking back at a Jerusalem that has already been destroyed. This is consistent with the editorial layers of the book.
19: Pharaoh king of Egypt, his servants, his officials, all his people, and all the foreign mix; all the kings of the land of Uz and all the kings of the land of the Philistines (Ashkelon, Gaza, Ekron, and the remnant of Ashdod). The cup-list encompasses Egypt (the southern power Judah sought as an ally against Babylon), the land of Uz (associated with Job — the eastern desert region), and the Philistine cities. The geographic sweep moves from Judah's immediate neighbors to more distant nations, building toward the comprehensive judgment of all nations.
20: The nations receiving the cup of wrath continue: Edom, Moab, the sons of Ammon — the Transjordanian neighbors whose complex relationship with Israel included ancient kinship (Edom = Esau, Moab and Ammon = Lot's sons) alongside persistent hostility. Their inclusion in the cup-judgment is significant: covenantal proximity does not confer immunity. All the kings of Tyre, all the kings of Sidon, and the kings of the coastland across the sea — the Phoenician maritime cities that controlled Mediterranean trade.
21: The cup-list continues across the eastern and southern regions: Dedan, Tema, Buz, and all who cut the corners of their hair — Arabian tribes and territories. The description who cut the corners of their hair (qotzei pe'ah) refers to a distinctive desert-tribe practice of shaving the temples, mentioned in Herodotus and associated with various Arabian groups. The Torah prohibited this practice for Israelites (Leviticus 19:27) precisely because of its association with non-Israelite religious customs.
22: All the kings of Arabia and all the kings of the mixed tribes who dwell in the desert; all the kings of Zimri, all the kings of Elam, and all the kings of Media. The cup-list moves east: Elam (southwestern Iran, east of Babylon) and Media (northwestern Iran) — both of which will eventually be part of the coalition that overthrows Babylon (cf. Isaiah 13:17: I will stir up the Medes against them). The inclusion of Babylon's future destroyers in the cup-list anticipates the reversal of fortunes that chapters 50–51 will develop.
23: All the kings of the north, far and near, one after another, and all the kingdoms of the world that are on the face of the earth. And after them the king of Babylon shall drink. The cup-list culminates with the global sweep — all kingdoms of the world — and then Babylon itself. The sequential structure is important: Babylon is the last to drink, having served as the instrument of YHWH's judgment on all the others. Babylon's cup comes after it has executed the others' judgment. The structure mirrors the oracles against the nations in chapters 46–51, where Babylon occupies the longest and culminating section.
24: The nations listed span the full known world of the 7th-century BCE Near East: Egypt, Arabia, Philistia, Phoenicia, Transjordan, Persia, Media. The comprehensiveness of the list communicates YHWH's universal sovereignty. He is not a regional deity whose judgment is limited to his own territory; his cup of wrath reaches every kingdom under heaven.
25: The cup-list continues with more specific identifications of Arabian and desert-fringe peoples: Zimri (possibly in Arabia), the kings of Elam, and the extensive listing builds the picture of a global judgment that leaves no nation exempt. The theological claim embedded in the cup-oracle is the same claim Jeremiah made in chapters 10 and 23: YHWH is the God of all nations, not merely of Israel.
26: And after them the king of Babylon shall drink — the culmination and reversal: the cup-bearer becomes the cup-drinker. The instrument of judgment becomes the recipient of judgment. Sheshach (an Atbash cipher for Babel/Babylon — substituting reversed alphabet letters) appears here and in Jeremiah 51:41. The cipher may have served originally as a form of discretion when writing about Babylon was politically dangerous, or as a literary device signaling that the readers are meant to decode the reference.
27: Then you shall say to them, Thus says YHWH of hosts, the God of Israel: Drink, be drunk and vomit, fall and rise no more, because of the sword that I am sending among you. The vomiting and falling-to-rise-no-more moves beyond the metaphor of disorienting intoxication to the full picture of those killed in battle — the nations struck down by the sword will not recover. Rise no more signals the permanence of the judgment on nations that resist YHWH's sovereign authority.
28: And if they refuse to accept the cup from your hand to drink, then you shall say to them, Thus says YHWH of hosts: You must drink! The nations' resistance to the cup-judgment is addressed: even if they refuse, they must drink. The sovereign compulsion is absolute — YHWH's judgment cannot be declined by will or political calculation. The rhetorical force anticipates the nations' response to Jeremiah's message: they will not want to accept the judgment-cup, but the announcement of it is itself the beginning of its execution.
29: For behold, I begin to work disaster at the city that is called by my name, and shall you go unpunished? You shall not go unpunished, for I am summoning a sword against all the inhabitants of the earth, declares YHWH of hosts. The theological logic of universal judgment: if YHWH judges the city that bears his name — Jerusalem, the most privileged and protected place on earth — then no other city or nation can claim exemption. The judgment of his own people is the proof that YHWH's standard is universal, not tribal. Paul uses similar logic in Romans 2:1–11: the divine impartiality that judged Israel applies equally to Gentiles.
30: You, therefore, shall prophesy against them all these words, and say to them: YHWH will roar from on high, and from his holy habitation utter his voice; he will roar mightily against his fold, and shout, like those who tread grapes, against all the inhabitants of the earth. The cosmic theophany of YHWH as the roaring lion (cf. Amos 1:2; Hosea 11:10; Joel 3:16 — YHWH roars from Zion) pictures the divine judge making his voice heard across the created order. The harvest-shout of the grape-treaders is a sound of both joy (harvest celebration) and violence (trampling) — YHWH treads the nations as a winepress (cf. Isaiah 63:1–6; Revelation 19:15).
31: The clamor will resound to the ends of the earth, for YHWH has an indictment against the nations; he is entering into judgment with all flesh, and the wicked he will put to the sword, declares YHWH. YHWH has an indictment (rib, a covenant lawsuit) against the nations — the legal-covenant framework applied universally. The ends of the earth indicates the scope is no longer national but cosmic. Judgment with all flesh: the entire biological category of mortal life is subject to YHWH's judicial evaluation. This is the most universalizing statement in a universalizing chapter.
32: Thus says YHWH of hosts: Behold, disaster is going forth from nation to nation, and a great tempest is stirring from the farthest parts of the earth. The disaster cascades: nation to nation, from the farthest ends of the earth toward the center. The military campaigns of the ancient world spread destruction in waves — Babylon conquers one nation, which destabilizes the next, which affects the next. YHWH is the choreographer of this historical cascade.
33: And those pierced by YHWH on that day shall extend from one end of the earth to the other. They shall not be lamented, or gathered, or buried; they shall be dung on the surface of the ground. The bodies of the slain scattered across the entire earth, unburied and unlamented, enacts the covenant curse at its most extreme (cf. Deuteronomy 28:26) on a global scale. The absence of burial — the most basic human funeral rite — signifies the total collapse of the social order that normally maintains covenant dignity even in death.
34: Wail, you shepherds, and cry out, and roll in ashes, you lords of the flock, for the days of your slaughter and dispersion have come, and you shall fall like a choice vessel. The shepherds (national leaders, kings) are called to mourn their own imminent destruction. Roll in ashes (hitpalashtem): ashes on the head was the most extreme public mourning gesture. Fall like a choice vessel: the contrast between the precious ceramic vessel and its fragility — the leaders thought themselves valuable and protected; they will shatter like fine pottery thrown down.
35: No refuge will remain for the shepherds, nor escape for the lords of the flock. The military situation is described in terms of total blockade: no escape routes, no refuges. The leaders who fed off their flocks rather than protecting them will find that neither their political resources nor their military alliances provide any escape from YHWH's judgment.
36: Hark! The cry of the shepherds, and the wail of the lords of the flock! For YHWH is laying waste their pasture. The cry of the leaders is heard — not the triumphant cry of victory but the wailing of those whose domain is being destroyed. YHWH is laying waste their pasture: the pastures that the leaders controlled (their nations, territories, spheres of influence) are devastated. The shepherd-leaders lose the very lands they exploited.
37: And the peaceful folds are devastated because of the fierce anger of YHWH. Peaceful folds (nevot hashalom) — the prosperous, settled territories that once appeared to be under YHWH's blessing. The fierce anger (charon af) — the burning anger of YHWH's covenant indignation — is the force that transforms peaceful prosperity into waste. The transformation is sudden and total.
38: Like a lion he has left his lair, for their land has become a waste because of the sword of the oppressor, and because of his fierce anger. The lion-leaving-his-lair image pictures YHWH as a predator emerging from concealment to attack. The oppressor's sword (Babylon, YHWH's instrument) and YHWH's fierce anger operate together — human military action and divine wrath are two aspects of the same judgment event. The chapter ends with the land laid waste: the covenant land that was YHWH's gift has become a wasteland through covenant violation and divine response.
